text.title: Reference Program — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every reference program must enforce. Violating any one of these leaks goodwill, burns clients, or produces unconvincing social proof.

rule.statement: Every reference request MUST offer the client a concrete value in return — peer introduction, training credit, public co-marketing slot, charity donation, or commercial discount. Pure-ask is forbidden.

rationale.source: Reichheld — The Ultimate Question 2.0; Bain customer-loyalty research

rule.rationale: References that are asked without an offer produce one-shot compliance and resentment. References that are exchanged become a recurring relationship asset. The exchange does not have to be expensive — a peer intro to a senior contact in the client's own target market is high-value at near-zero cost.

rule.statement: No single reference is contacted more than 2 times per quarter (peer calls, case-study updates, podcast asks combined). Hard cap; no exceptions for must-win deals.

rationale.source: Forrester reference-selling research; field interviews with B2B sales leaders

rule.rationale: Reference fatigue is the silent killer of programs. Clients stop returning calls after the 3rd ask; the relationship turns from advocacy into chore. The cap forces the program to maintain ≥3-deep reference depth per archetype rather than burning a single star reference.

rule.statement: A reference MUST be matched to the prospect by buyer-archetype (industry, company-size band, role of decision-maker, deal-shape). Generic "any happy client" routing is forbidden.

rationale.source: GTM playbook canon (Winning by Design, Force Management)

rule.rationale: Cross-archetype references convert at <30% the rate of matched references because the prospect cannot self-identify. A SaaS founder talking to a manufacturing CIO does not move the deal. The registry MUST tag every reference with at least industry, headcount band, and decision-maker title.

rule.statement: Every reference asset (logo, quote, case study, peer-call availability) MUST have an explicit written consent record dated within the last 12 months, with a `permitted_uses` list (website, deck, peer-call, podcast, paid ads, etc.).

rationale.source: GDPR / CCPA practice for B2B endorsements; case law on unauthorised logo use

rule.rationale: Implicit consent ("they didn't object") fails at the worst moment. Logo on a paid ad without consent triggers cease-and-desist letters from procurement at large clients. The consent record turns "I think they're fine with it" into a date-stamped, scoped permission.

rule.statement: Reference assets carry an explicit `expires_on` (12 months from consent unless renewed) AND a `freshness_check` cadence. Expired assets MUST be removed from sales decks and website within 30 days.

rationale.source: Field practice; legal risk of unauthorised use

rule.rationale: Clients churn, sponsors leave, products change. A 4-year-old logo on the homepage when the company is now using a competitor is embarrassing at best and legally actionable at worst. Expiry forces deliberate renewal.

example.title: Value-exchange offer in the ask
example.code: "Hi Maria, we're closing a deal with a fintech that looks a lot like where you were 18 months ago. Would you be open to a 20-min peer call with their CTO next week? In exchange, I'd like to introduce you to Sasha, who runs platform-engineering at <bigger fintech> — she's the exact persona you said you wanted to meet last quarter. No pressure either way."
example.note: Illustrates r1 (concrete value-exchange), r3 (archetype match between Maria's company and the prospect), r4 (peer-call as the asset, easy to consent).

example.title: Reference burn pattern
example.code: Q1: Maria does a peer call with prospect A. Q1: Maria's quote used in new case study without renewed consent. Q1: Maria asked for a podcast appearance. Q1: Maria asked to speak at user conference. → Maria stops returning emails by Q2.
example.note: r2 violation (≥4 asks in one quarter), r4 violation (quote reused without renewed consent). The relationship is now worth zero references.
